name: Workstation Physical Presence Timeline
description: Reconstructs a forensic-grade 'who was physically at this machine and when' timeline by fusing lock/unlock events (4800/4801), screensaver idle markers (4802/4803), interactive-only logon types (4624 Type 2/7), system boot and shutdown signals (6005/6006/6008), sleep/hibernate entry (Kernel-Power 42), lid-or-button triggered sleep, and system resume with wake-source attribution (Power-Troubleshooter 1, Kernel-Power 107). Unlike the Logon/Logoff profile this focuses exclusively on PHYSICAL console presence — not network sessions. Critical for HR investigations ('was the user actually working?'), stolen/lost laptop forensics, after-hours access, unattended machine incidents, compliance audits, and insider threat timelines. EID 4803 (screensaver dismissed) is the only Windows event that proves physical keyboard/mouse input at the console. Kernel-Power EID 42 with 'Sleep Reason: Button or Lid' proves the laptop lid was closed. Power-Troubleshooter EID 1 with WakeSource='Lid' proves the laptop lid was reopened.
author: Community — Day-to-Day HR/Compliance/Forensics

event_descriptions.4624: Account logged on — FILTER BY LogonType: Type 2 = fresh interactive console logon (someone sat down and typed their password or PIN); Type 7 = workstation unlock (same presence signal as 4801 but includes username in Subject); Type 10 = RemoteInteractive (RDP — NOT physical presence, see 4778); Type 11 = CachedInteractive (domain creds cached, machine was offline). Ignore Type 3 (network), Type 4 (batch), Type 5 (service). SubjectUserName + WorkstationName + LogonType together tell you WHO sat at WHICH machine. LogonID field links this event to all subsequent activity in the same session.
event_descriptions.4634: Account logged off — session terminated. Paired with 4624 Type 2/7 to measure how long a user was actively logged in. Note: 4634 may not fire reliably for interactive sessions; 4647 (user-initiated logoff) is a stronger presence termination signal.
event_descriptions.4647: User-initiated logoff — the user explicitly chose Log Off from the Start menu or used 'logoff' command. Stronger presence termination signal than 4634 (which can be automatic/background). Confirms the user was at the machine and made a deliberate choice to leave. Timestamp = last confirmed moment of intentional presence.
event_descriptions.4778: Session RECONNECTED to window station — a disconnected session was resumed. For Terminal Services/RDP, this is a remote reconnect (NOT physical presence — check SessionName: if 'RDP-Tcp' it is remote; if 'Console' it may be Fast User Switch). For console, indicates Fast User Switching reconnect. Always inspect the SessionName and ClientAddress fields.
event_descriptions.4779: Session DISCONNECTED from window station — active session disconnected without full logoff. For RDP: user closed the Remote Desktop window. For console: Fast User Switching occurred. If this fires on a standalone non-RDP workstation, investigate — it should not occur. Repeated 4779 without 4778 on a non-server suggests Fast User Switching is being abused for account hopping.
event_descriptions.4800: Workstation LOCKED — user pressed Win+L, chose Lock from Start menu, or auto-lock policy triggered (e.g., screen timeout, Group Policy idle lock). Subject.AccountName = who locked it. Timestamp = machine left unattended from this point. Pair with the chronologically next 4801 to measure the exact unattended window. If EID 42 (sleep) follows within seconds, the user also closed the lid.
event_descriptions.4801: Workstation UNLOCKED — someone authenticated at the physical console to dismiss the lock screen via password, PIN, Windows Hello, or smart card. Subject.AccountName = who unlocked it. If a DIFFERENT account unlocked versus who locked (4800), investigate immediately: someone else accessed this workstation. This cross-account unlock is a critical insider threat signal.
event_descriptions.4802: Screen saver INVOKED — the machine was idle for the configured screen-saver timeout period with no keyboard or mouse input. No one was actively using the machine from this point. Timestamp marks the start of an idle/unattended window. Compute exact idle duration by finding the next 4803 event. Note: 4802 does NOT fire on modern Windows when no screen saver is configured (plain blank screen timeout does not log this).
event_descriptions.4803: Screen saver DISMISSED — *** HIGH-VALUE PHYSICAL PRESENCE INDICATOR *** Physical keyboard or mouse input occurred at the console. This event CANNOT be generated remotely — it requires local physical input to dismiss the screen saver. Use this as irrefutable proof of physical presence at a specific timestamp. If 4803 fires at 2 AM on a weekend, someone was physically at that machine. Pair with 4802 to compute how long the machine was idle before someone interacted with it.
event_descriptions.42: *** LID CLOSE / SLEEP ENTRY *** Source: Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Power in System.evtx. The system is entering sleep (ACPI S3 or Modern Standby S0ix). Check the 'Sleep Reason' field in the EventData XML: 'Button or Lid' = user closed the laptop lid or pressed the power button — highest-confidence physical departure indicator; 'System Idle' = unattended machine hit power-plan idle timeout; 'Application API' = software-triggered sleep (less interesting forensically). Pair with the next Kernel-Power EID 107 or Power-Troubleshooter EID 1 to compute exact sleep duration. On laptops, repeated 'Button or Lid' events outside business hours are strong forensic markers.
event_descriptions.107: SYSTEM RESUMED FROM SLEEP — Source: Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Power in System.evtx. Logged when the system wakes from sleep. Timestamp is the wake time recorded by the kernel clock (may drift slightly vs real-time due to clock sync delays). Cross-reference with Power-Troubleshooter EID 1 for the more accurate SleepTime/WakeTime pair and the human-readable WakeSource field. Repeated 107 events at unexpected hours indicate the machine was physically accessed or remotely wake-triggered.
event_descriptions.41: *** CRITICAL — UNEXPECTED DIRTY SHUTDOWN *** Source: Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Power in System.evtx. Level=Critical. The system did not shut down cleanly — power was forcibly cut (pulled plug, held power button, battery removed, crash). BugCheckCode=0 = no crash dump, just forced power loss. A non-zero BugCheckCode = BSOD. In laptop theft investigations: 41 with no preceding 4800/42/6006 means someone yanked the power. Multiple 41 events on a corporate asset are also a tamper/damage signal. In HR investigations: 41 during working hours with no crash could mean 'rage quit' forced power-off.
event_descriptions.12: OS START TIME — Source: Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-General in System.evtx. The operating system has started at the time indicated. Provides the authoritative boot timestamp. Use to anchor the beginning of each session's timeline. Note: EID 12 logs the OS kernel start; the actual user login will follow minutes later via 4624. Time between EID 12 and first 4624 Type 2 = boot-to-login duration, useful for detecting tampering or slow boot anomalies.
event_descriptions.13: OS SHUTDOWN INITIATED — Source: Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-General in System.evtx. The OS shutdown sequence was initiated. This is the last kernel event before system halt. Pairing EID 12 (start) and EID 13 (shutdown) gives you the machine's total uptime window per session, independent of user logon activity. A machine that stays on 24/7 with no EID 13 events has never been cleanly shut down — it may have been only sleeping (EID 42).
event_descriptions.6005: EVENT LOG SERVICE STARTED = SYSTEM BOOT — Source: EventLog in System.evtx. The Windows Event Log service started, which happens at system boot. This is the earliest event-log-level indicator of a fresh OS boot (EID 12 may precede it but comes from a different source). Use as boot anchor. In stolen laptop investigations, this event — combined with its timestamp — tells you when the laptop was powered on after theft.
event_descriptions.6006: EVENT LOG SERVICE STOPPED = CLEAN SHUTDOWN — Source: EventLog in System.evtx. The Event Log service is stopping as part of a clean, orderly shutdown initiated by the user or OS. Absence of 6006 before the next 6005 means the previous session ended with an unexpected shutdown (look for 6008 or Kernel-Power 41). Together 6005/6006 pairs build a complete on/off timeline independent of who was logged in.
event_descriptions.6008: *** UNEXPECTED/DIRTY SHUTDOWN DETECTED *** Source: EventLog in System.evtx. Logged at the NEXT boot after an unexpected shutdown — Windows detected that the last shutdown was not clean. The event timestamp is the CURRENT boot time, not the crash time; look at Kernel-Power EID 41 for the actual crash/power-loss moment. In HR investigations: repeated 6008 on a desktop may indicate physical tampering. In theft forensics: a 6008 after a gap in the timeline confirms the device lost power abruptly (battery drained or forcible shutdown by a thief).
event_descriptions.1: SYSTEM RETURNED FROM LOW-POWER STATE — Source: Microsoft-Windows-Power-Troubleshooter in System.evtx. This is the richest wake event in Windows. EventData contains: SleepTime (exact UTC timestamp when system entered sleep), WakeTime (exact UTC timestamp when system woke), SleepDuration (seconds asleep), WakeSource (the device or trigger that woke the system — 'Lid' = laptop lid was physically opened, 'Power Button' = power button pressed, 'Timer' = scheduled task or maintenance, 'Network' = Wake-on-LAN). *** 'WakeSource: Lid' is irrefutable proof a human opened the laptop lid at that exact moment. *** Correlate WakeTime with 4801 (unlock) and 4803 (screensaver dismissed) within the next 60 seconds to build a complete presence chain.
